teksStandardTitle: Evaluate the impact of artificial intelligence on society and everyday life
subject: visionary
title: How Does a Computer Think?
description: Explore how AI makes decisions and what it means for a machine to 'learn'.

content.prompt: Read the passage about how computers learn.
content.passageText: Imagine teaching a dog a new trick. You show the dog what to do, reward it when it gets it right, and repeat until the dog learns. Artificial Intelligence (AI) works the same way — but with data instead of treats. When engineers build a face-recognition app, they show it thousands of photos labeled 'face' or 'not a face.' The computer finds patterns in those photos and uses them to recognize new faces it has never seen before. This process is called machine learning, and it's the engine behind everything from voice assistants to spam filters.

comprehensionQuestions.question: What do engineers show an AI when teaching it to recognize faces?
What is the process where a computer finds patterns in data called?

content.instruction: Read carefully, then answer the questions.

content.prompt: Which of the following is an example of AI making a decision?
options: A streaming app suggesting a show you might like
content.instruction: Think about which answer shows a computer learning from your behavior.

content.prompt: A self-driving car uses AI to stay in its lane. What kind of data does it most likely use?
content.instruction: Think about what information a car would need to 'see' the road.

explorerContent.title: Train Your Own Classifier
explorerContent.instruction: Sort the items below into 'Animal' or 'Machine.' Imagine you are teaching an AI to tell the difference.

teksStandardTitle: Apply computational thinking to design and program a robot to solve a real-world problem
subject: visionary
title: Robots That Rescue
description: Discover how robots are programmed to help people in dangerous situations.

content.prompt: Read about how robots are used in disaster relief.
content.passageText: When a building collapses after an earthquake, sending humans inside is extremely dangerous. That's where rescue robots come in. Engineers at MIT built a snake-like robot that can squeeze through rubble and send back video of survivors. These robots are programmed with a sequence of instructions — move forward 30cm, turn left 15 degrees, scan with thermal camera — repeated over and over. The robot doesn't think on its own; it follows a precise program. But the camera data it collects helps human operators make life-saving decisions.

comprehensionQuestions.question: Why do engineers send robots into collapsed buildings instead of people?
options: It is too dangerous for humans

comprehensionQuestions.question: What does the snake robot's thermal camera help detect?

content.instruction: Read the passage and answer the questions below.

content.prompt: A robot is given this program: [move forward 1m, turn right 90°, repeat 4 times]. What shape does it trace?
content.instruction: Try drawing it out — each step turns the robot 90 degrees.

content.prompt: Which programming concept is used when a robot repeats the same instruction multiple times?
content.instruction: Think about what it's called when code runs again and again.

explorerContent.title: Program the Rescue Bot
explorerContent.instruction: Write 4 instructions to guide a robot from Start to the Survivor. Use: F (forward), L (turn left), R (turn right).

teksStandardTitle: Collect, organize, and analyze data using appropriate digital tools and present findings
subject: visionary
title: Numbers That Tell Stories
description: Learn how data scientists find patterns and share discoveries with the world.

content.prompt: Read about how data scientists work.
content.passageText: In 1854, London was struck by a devastating cholera outbreak. Dr. John Snow didn't have a computer, but he had data. He plotted every death on a map and noticed they all clustered around one water pump on Broad Street. By analyzing the pattern, he proved that dirty water — not 'bad air' — caused cholera. Today, data scientists do the same thing with much larger datasets. They collect millions of data points, clean out errors, look for patterns, and use those patterns to make predictions. Data science has helped predict flu outbreaks, improve cancer treatment, and even make movies you're likely to enjoy.

comprehensionQuestions.question: What did Dr. John Snow use to figure out the source of the cholera outbreak?
What do data scientists do AFTER they collect data?

content.prompt: A data scientist records daily temperatures for 5 days: 72°, 68°, 74°, 70°, 76°. What is the average temperature?
content.instruction: Add all temperatures, then divide by the number of days.

content.prompt: A bar chart shows that 3rd graders read an average of 20 books per year, and 4th graders read 25. What can a data scientist conclude?
options: Reading tends to increase as students get older
Both grades read the same amount
content.instruction: Look for trends — what changes as students get older?

explorerContent.title: Mini Data Challenge
explorerContent.instruction: You surveyed 10 friends about their favorite colors: Red-3, Blue-4, Green-2, Yellow-1. What percentage chose Blue?

teksStandardTitle: Analyze ethical issues related to artificial intelligence including bias, privacy, and fairness
subject: visionary
title: When Algorithms Are Unfair
description: Explore how AI can accidentally be biased and why engineers must build fair systems.

content.prompt: Read about AI bias and fairness.
content.passageText: In 2018, researchers at MIT discovered that a popular facial recognition AI made errors more than 34% of the time when identifying darker-skinned women — but only 1% of the time for lighter-skinned men. Why? Because the AI was trained mostly on photos of lighter-skinned people. It learned well what it saw most. This is called 'algorithmic bias' — when an AI reflects unfairness that exists in its training data. Bias in AI can have real consequences: unfair loan denials, incorrect criminal records, or medical tools that work less well for certain groups. The solution starts with diverse data, diverse engineering teams, and asking hard questions about fairness before deploying AI systems.

comprehensionQuestions.question: Why did the facial recognition AI perform worse on darker-skinned women?
options: The AI was trained mostly on lighter-skinned faces
The AI was not connected to the internet

comprehensionQuestions.question: What is one way engineers can reduce algorithmic bias?
options: Only train AI on math problems

content.instruction: Think critically as you read. This topic has real-world consequences.

content.prompt: An AI used to approve job applications was trained only on data from one company that historically hired mostly men. What is the most likely problem?
options: The AI will work too slowly
The AI may unfairly reject qualified women
The AI will not understand resumes
The AI will hire too many people
content.instruction: Think about what the AI 'learned' from the historical data.

content.prompt: Which of the following best describes 'algorithmic bias'?
options: An AI that runs too slowly on old computers
Unfair outcomes caused by problems in training data or design
A bug that crashes the program
An AI that prefers one programming language
content.instruction: Choose the definition that connects bias to the AI's decisions.

explorerContent.title: Design Your Fair AI
explorerContent.instruction: Imagine you are building an AI that decides who gets a scholarship. Write 3 rules you would give it to make sure it's fair.

payload.storyPrompt: My Fair Scholarship AI would follow these 3 rules...

teksStandardTitle: Create an algorithm and trace its execution to solve a problem
subject: visionary
title: Algorithms: Step-by-Step Thinking
description: Understand what algorithms are and how they power everything from search engines to GPS.

content.prompt: Read about algorithms in daily life.
content.passageText: Every time you search for something online, a powerful algorithm runs in milliseconds to decide which results to show you first. An algorithm is simply a set of step-by-step instructions that solves a problem — like a recipe. When Google's PageRank algorithm was invented in 1998, it revolutionized the internet by ranking pages based on how many other trusted pages linked to them. Before that, search results were random. Algorithms are everywhere: GPS uses Dijkstra's algorithm to find the shortest route, streaming apps use recommendation algorithms to find shows, and hospitals use diagnostic algorithms to help doctors.

comprehensionQuestions.question: What is an algorithm?
options: A step-by-step set of instructions that solves a problem

comprehensionQuestions.question: Which algorithm does your GPS use to find the shortest route?

content.prompt: You want to sort [5, 2, 8, 1, 6] from smallest to largest. What is the first number in the sorted list?
content.instruction: Find the smallest number in the list.

content.prompt: Which step comes LAST when a GPS finds your route?
options: Display the fastest route on screen
content.instruction: Think about the logical order of steps.

explorerContent.title: Trace the Algorithm
explorerContent.instruction: Given the list [8, 3, 6, 1, 4], trace through a bubble sort algorithm. What does the list look like after 2 full passes?
